lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: If but as well I other accents borrow,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: That can my speech defuse, my good intent

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: May carry through itself to that full issue

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: For which I razed my likeness. Now, banish’d Kent,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: If thou canst serve where thou dost stand condemn’d,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: So may it come, thy master, whom thou lovest,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Shall find thee full of labours.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Let me not stay a jot for dinner; go get it ready.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: A man, sir.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: What dost thou profess? what wouldst thou with us?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I do profess to be no less than I seem; to serve

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: him truly that will put me in trust: to love him that

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: is honest; to converse with him that is wise, and says little;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: to fear judgment; to fight when I cannot choose; and to eat

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: no fish.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: What art thou?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: A very honest-hearted fellow, and as poor as the king.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: If thou be as poor for a subject as he is for

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: a king, thou art poor enough. What wouldst thou?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Service.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Who wouldst thou serve?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: You.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Dost thou know me, fellow?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: No, sir; but you have that in your countenance which I would fain call master.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: What’s that?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Authority.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: What services canst thou do?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I can keep honest counsel, ride, run, mar a curious tale in

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: telling it, and deliver a plain message bluntly: that which ordinary men

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: are fit for, I am qualified in; and the best of me

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: is diligence.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: How old art thou?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Not so young, sir, to love a woman for singing, nor so

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: old to dote on her for any thing: I have years on

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: my back forty eight.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Follow me; thou shalt serve me: if I like thee no worse

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: after dinner, I will not part from thee yet. Dinner, ho, dinner!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Where’s my knave? my fool? Go you, and call my fool hither.

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: So please you—

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: What says the fellow there? Call the clotpoll back.

lines.speaker: KNIGHT
lines.text: He says, my lord, your daughter is not well.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Why came not the slave back to me when I called him.

lines.speaker: KNIGHT
lines.text: Sir, he answered me in the roundest manner, he would not.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: He would not!

lines.speaker: KNIGHT
lines.text: My lord, I know not what the matter is; but, to my

lines.speaker: KNIGHT
lines.text: judgment, your highness is not entertained with that ceremonious affection as you

lines.speaker: KNIGHT
lines.text: were wont; there’s a great abatement of kindness appears as well in

lines.speaker: KNIGHT
lines.text: the general dependants as in the duke himself also and your daughter.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Ha! sayest thou so?

lines.speaker: KNIGHT
lines.text: I beseech you, pardon me, my lord, if I be mistaken; for

lines.speaker: KNIGHT
lines.text: my duty cannot be silent when I think your highness wronged.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Thou but rememberest me of mine own conception: I have perceived a

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: most faint neglect of late; which I have rather blamed as mine

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: own jealous curiosity than as a very pretence and purpose of unkindness:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I will look further into’t. But where’s my fool? I have not

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: seen him this two days.

lines.speaker: KNIGHT
lines.text: Since my young lady’s going into France, sir, the fool hath much pined away.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: No more of that; I have noted it well. Go you, and

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: tell my daughter I would speak with her. Go you, call hither

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: my fool.

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: My lady’s father.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: “My lady’s father”! my lord’s knave: your whoreson dog! you slave! you cur!

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: I am none of these, my lord; I beseech your pardon.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Do you bandy looks with me, you rascal? Striking him.

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: I’ll not be struck, my lord.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Nor tripped neither, you base football player. Tripping up his heels.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I thank thee, fellow; thou servest me, and I’ll love thee.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Come, sir, arise, away! I’ll teach you differences: away, away! if you

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: will measure your lubber’s length again, tarry: but away! go to; have

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: you wisdom? so. Pushes Oswald out.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Now, my friendly knave, I thank thee: there’s earnest of thy service.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Let me hire him too: here’s my coxcomb.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: How now, my pretty knave! how dost thou?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Sirrah, you were best take my coxcomb.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Why, fool?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Why, for taking one’s part that’s out of favour: nay, an thou

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: canst not smile as the wind sits, thou’lt catch cold shortly: there,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: take my coxcomb: why, this fellow has banished two on’s daughters, and

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: did the third a blessing against his will; if thou follow him,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: thou must needs wear my coxcomb. How now, nuncle! Would I had

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: two coxcombs and two daughters!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Why, my boy?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: If I gave them all my living, I’ld keep my coxcombs myself.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: There’s mine; beg another of thy daughters.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Take heed, sirrah; the whip.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Truth’s a dog must to kennel; he must be whipped out, when

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Lady the brach may stand by the fire and stink.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: A pestilent gall to me!

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Sirrah, I’ll teach thee a speech.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Do.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Mark it, nuncle:

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Have more than thou showest,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Speak less than thou knowest,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Lend less than thou owest,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Ride more than thou goest,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Learn more than thou trowest,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Set less than thou throwest;

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Leave thy drink and thy whore,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: And keep in-a-door,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: And thou shalt have more

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Than two tens to a score.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: This is nothing, fool.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Then ’tis like the breath of an unfee’d lawyer; you gave me

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: nothing for’t. Can you make no use of nothing, nuncle?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Why, no, boy; nothing can be made out of nothing.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: To Kent. Prithee, tell him, so much the rent of his land

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: comes to: he will not believe a fool.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: A bitter fool!

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Dost thou know the difference, my boy, between a bitter fool and a sweet fool?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: No, lad; teach me.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: That lord that counsell’d thee

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: To give away thy land,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Come place him here by me,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Do thou for him stand:

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: The sweet and bitter fool

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Will presently appear;

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: The one in motley here,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: The other found out there.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Dost thou call me fool, boy?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: All thy other titles thou hast given away; that thou wast born with.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: This is not altogether fool, my lord.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: No, faith, lords and great men will not let me; if I

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: had a monopoly out, they would have part on’t: and ladies too,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: they will not let me have all fool to myself; they’ll be

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: snatching. Give me an egg, nuncle, and I’ll give thee two crowns.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: What two crowns shall they be?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Why, after I have cut the egg i’ the middle, and eat

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: up the meat, the two crowns of the egg. When thou clovest

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: thy crown i’ the middle, and gavest away both parts, thou borest

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: thy ass on thy back o’er the dirt: thou hadst little wit

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: in thy bald crown, when thou gavest thy golden one away. If

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: I speak like myself in this, let him be whipped that first

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: finds it so.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: When were you wont to be so full of songs, sirrah?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: I have used it, nuncle, ever since thou madest thy daughters thy

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: mothers: for when thou gavest them the rod, and put’st down thine

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: own breeches,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: An you lie, sirrah, we’ll have you whipped.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: I marvel what kin thou and thy daughters are: they’ll have me

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: whipped for speaking true, thou’lt have me whipped for lying; and sometimes

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: I am whipped for holding my peace. I had rather be any

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: kind o’ thing than a fool: and yet I would not be

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: thee, nuncle; thou hast pared thy wit o’ both sides, and left

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: nothing i’ the middle: here comes one o’ the parings.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: How now, daughter! what makes that frontlet on? Methinks you are too

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: much of late i’ the frown.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Thou wast a pretty fellow when thou hadst no need to care

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: for her frowning; now thou art an O without a figure: I

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: am better than thou art now; I am a fool, thou art

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: nothing. To Goneril. Yes, forsooth, I will hold my tongue; so your

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: face bids me, though you say nothing.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Not only, sir, this your all-licensed fool,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: But other of your insolent retinue

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Do hourly carp and quarrel; breaking forth

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: In rank and not-to-be endured riots. Sir,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: I had thought, by making this well known unto you,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: To have found a safe redress; but now grow fearful,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: By what yourself too late have spoke and done.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: That you protect this course, and put it on

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: By your allowance; which if you should, the fault

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Would not ’scape censure, nor the redresses sleep,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Which, in the tender of a wholesome weal,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Might in their working do you that offence,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Which else were shame, that then necessity

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Will call discreet proceeding.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: For, you trow, nuncle,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: The hedge-sparrow fed the cuckoo so long,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: That it’s had it head bit off by it young.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: So, out went the candle, and we were left darkling.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Are you our daughter?

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Come, sir,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: I would you would make use of that good wisdom,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Whereof I know you are fraught; and put away

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: These dispositions, that of late transform you

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: From what you rightly are.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: May not an ass know when the cart draws the horse? Whoop,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Jug! I love thee.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Doth any here know me? This is not Lear:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Doth Lear walk thus? speak thus? Where are his eyes?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Either his notion weakens, his discernings

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Are lethargied—Ha! waking? ’tis not so.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Who is it that can tell me who I am?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Lear’s shadow.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I would learn that; for, by the marks of sovereignty, knowledge, and

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: reason, I should be false persuaded I had daughters.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Which they will make an obedient father.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Your name, fair gentlewoman?

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: This admiration, sir, is much o’ the savour

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Of other your new pranks. I do beseech you

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: To understand my purposes aright:

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: As you are old and reverend, you should be wise.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Here do you keep a hundred knights and squires;

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Men so disorder’d, so debosh’d and bold,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: That this our court, infected with their manners,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Shows like a riotous inn: epicurism and lust

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Make it more like a tavern or a brothel

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Than a graced palace. The shame itself doth speak

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: For instant remedy: be then desired

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: By her, that else will take the thing she begs,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: A little to disquantity your train;

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: And the remainder, that shall still depend,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: To be such men as may besort your age,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: And know themselves and you.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Darkness and devils!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Saddle my horses; call my train together:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Degenerate bastard! I’ll not trouble thee.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Yet have I left a daughter.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: You strike my people; and your disorder’d rabble

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Make servants of their betters.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Woe, that too late repents—

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To Albany. O, sir, are you come?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Is it your will? Speak, sir. Prepare my horses.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Ingratitude, thou marble-hearted fiend,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: More hideous when thou show’st thee in a child

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Than the sea-monster!

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Pray, sir, be patient.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To Goneril. Detested kite! thou liest.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: My train are men of choice and rarest parts,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That all particulars of duty know,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And in the most exact regard support

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The worships of their name. O most small fault,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: How ugly didst thou in Cordelia show!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That, like an engine, wrench’d my frame of nature

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: From the fix’d place; drew from heart all love,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And added to the gall. O Lear, Lear, Lear!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Beat at this gate, that let thy folly in, Striking his head.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And thy dear judgment out! Go, go, my people.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: My lord, I am guiltless, as I am ignorant

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Of what hath moved you.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: It may be so, my lord.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Hear, nature, hear; dear goddess, hear!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Suspend thy purpose, if thou didst intend

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To make this creature fruitful!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Into her womb convey sterility!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Dry up in her the organs of increase;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And from her derogate body never spring

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: A babe to honour her! If she must teem,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Create her child of spleen; that it may live,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And be a thwart disnatured torment to her!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Let it stamp wrinkles in her brow of youth;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: With cadent tears fret channels in her cheeks;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Turn all her mother’s pains and benefits

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To laughter and contempt; that she may feel

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: How sharper than a serpent’s tooth it is

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To have a thankless child! Away, away!

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Now, gods that we adore, whereof comes this?

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Never afflict yourself to know the cause;

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: But let his disposition have that scope

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: That dotage gives it.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: What, fifty of my followers at a clap!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Within a fortnight!

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: What’s the matter, sir?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I’ll tell thee: To Goneril.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Life and death! I am ashamed

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That thou hast power to shake my manhood thus;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That these hot tears, which break from me perforce,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Should make thee worth them. Blasts and fogs upon thee!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The untented woundings of a father’s curse

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Pierce every sense about thee! Old fond eyes,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Beweep this cause again, I’ll pluck ye out,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: And cast you, with the waters that you lose,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To temper clay. Yea, it is come to this?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Let it be so: yet have I left a daughter,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Who, I am sure, is kind and comfortable:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: When she shall hear this of thee, with her nails

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: She’ll flay thy wolvish visage. Thou shalt find

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That I’ll resume the shape which thou dost think

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I have cast off for ever: thou shalt,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I warrant thee.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Do you mark that, my lord?

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: I cannot be so partial, Goneril,

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: To the great love I bear you—

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Pray you, content. What, Oswald, ho! To the Fool. You, sir, more

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: knave than fool, after your master.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Nuncle Lear, nuncle Lear, tarry and take the fool

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: with thee.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: A fox, when one has caught her,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: And such a daughter,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Should sure to the slaughter,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: If my cap would buy a halter:

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: So the fool follows after.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: This man hath had good counsel:—a hundred knights!

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: ’Tis politic and safe to let him keep

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: At point a hundred knights: yes, that, on every dream,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Each buzz, each fancy, each complaint, dislike,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: He may enguard his dotage with their powers,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: And hold our lives in mercy. Oswald, I say!

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Well, you may fear too far.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Safer than trust too far:

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Let me still take away the harms I fear,

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Not fear still to be taken: I know his heart.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: What he hath utter’d I have writ my sister

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: If she sustain him and his hundred knights

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: When I have show’d the unfitness—

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: Yes, madam.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Take you some company, and away to horse:

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Inform her full of my particular fear;

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: And thereto add such reasons of your own

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: As may compact it more. Get you gone;

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: And hasten your return.

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: How far your eyes may pierce I can not tell:

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Striving to better, oft we mar what’s well.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Nay, then—

lines.speaker: ALBANY
lines.text: Well, well; the event.

stage_directions.text: Horns within. Enter King Lear, Knights, and Attendants.
You, you, sirrah, where’s my daughter?
Where’s my fool, ho? I think the world’s asleep.
O, you sir, you, come you hither, sir: who am I, sir?
Singing. Fools had ne’er less wit in a year; For wise men are grown foppish, They know not how their wits to wear, Their manners are so apish.
Singing. Then they for sudden joy did weep, And I for sorrow sung, That such a king should play bo-peep, And go the fools among.
Prithee, nuncle, keep a schoolmaster that can teach thy fool to lie: I would fain learn to lie.
Mum, mum, He that keeps nor crust nor crum, Weary of all, shall want some.
Pointing to King Lear. That’s a shealed peascod.
Exeunt King Lear, Kent, and Attendants.
How now, Oswald! What, have you writ that letter to my sister?
No, no, my lord, This milky gentleness and course of yours Though I condemn not, yet, under pardon, You are much more attask’d for want of wisdom Than praised for harmful mildness.

glosses.definition: Kent's plan to serve Lear anonymously
fellow — Lear's blunt address to the disguised Kent
the servant's new cold formula — Lear is no longer 'the king'
the cock's-comb cap of a licensed fool; by extension, a fool himself — the Fool's signature prop
contraction of 'mine uncle' — the Fool's affectionate nickname for Lear throughout the play
two fool's caps — the Fool offers one to Kent for following Lear
bitch; female hound (sometimes used as insult)
the Fool's pun — sweet fool / bitter fool
two halves of Lear's divided crown — the Fool's riddle
the hysterical passion; what Lear calls his rising grief
reduce in number — Goneril demands Lear cut his hundred knights
Lear's epithet for Goneril — she has degraded her lineage
terrible ocean beast — Goneril is worse
Cordelia's actual trivial fault, now immeasurably magnified in Lear's grief
demon with a hard-as-stone heart — Lear's name for ingratitude as incarnate evil
reproductive organs — Lear curses Goneril's fertility
unnatural agony — a child that rejects its nature
a poisonous bite — 'How sharper than a serpent's tooth it is / To have a thankless child!' (Lear's most-quoted line from this scene)
ungrateful offspring — the play's governing phrase
silent; hush — the Fool's teasing signal
Kent trips Oswald — football was a rough game of commoners
hated bird of prey — Lear on Goneril

glosses.phrase: the untented woundings of a father’s curse
glosses.definition: a father's curse as unhealable wounds

annotations.title: The Fool's first appearance — the license of truth-telling
annotations.body: The licensed fool was a historical office in European courts: a jester permitted to speak truths that would have been fatal from anyone else. Lear's Fool operates in that tradition at its most serious — he uses prose, riddle, song, and mock-prophecy to tell Lear truths the king cannot hear directly. 'Thou hadst little wit in thy bald crown when thou gav'st thy golden one away' is the whole play compressed into sixteen words. The Fool's genius is that he speaks while pretending not to mean it; his jester's cap is his armor. Shakespeare's deepest move is making the Fool's love for Lear unmistakable — he follows Lear into the storm when saner people leave. The licensed fool tradition ends when the fool starts caring; then he becomes a tragic figure, not a comic one.
sources: Enid Welsford, The Fool: His Social and Literary History (1935)
Robert Armin, Fool upon Fool (1605) — Armin played the original Fool

annotations.title: 'How sharper than a serpent's tooth' — Lear's first great curse
annotations.body: Goneril's demand that Lear reduce his retinue triggers the play's first great outburst: 'Ingratitude, thou marble-hearted fiend... / How sharper than a serpent's tooth it is / To have a thankless child!' The imagery is biblical (Lear compares Goneril to the sea-monster); the emotional logic is primitive — the organ of increase cursed with sterility, the mother's womb wished barren. Lear's rhetoric here is already crossing a line: he wishes Goneril's hypothetical child to be 'disnatured torment' to her, so she may know what a thankless child feels like. This is imprecation as tragic condition. The speech is where Goneril loses whatever sympathy modern productions sometimes try to give her: a father who would curse his daughter's womb has forfeited moral standing. But the play also shows she has already acted on the decision to break him — her rejection precedes his curse, not the reverse.
sources: Marilyn French, Shakespeare's Division of Experience (1981)

annotations.title: Albany begins to matter — the conscience the play will reward
annotations.body: Albany, Goneril's husband, says very little in 1.4. His 'How far your eyes may pierce I cannot tell' is the first whisper of moral hesitation from the play's patient-to-develop conscience. The contrast with Cornwall — instantly cruel, already drawn into the Edmund plot — will structure Act 3 and 4. By 4.2 Albany will be calling his wife 'most barbarous, most degenerate'; by 5.3 he will be on the right side of the final judgments. Shakespeare is doing something subtle: planting the quietest figure in Act 1 to be the surviving moral voice in Act 5. The technique is the reverse of Edmund, who dazzles in 1.2 and is dead by 5.3. Lear teaches that moral weight accumulates slowly in the people who speak least.

annotations.title: Lear's curse on Goneril's womb — the play's moral threshold
annotations.body: Lear's curse 'Into her womb convey sterility! / Dry up in her the organs of increase' takes imprecation to a place that Shakespeare elsewhere avoids. The father wishing his daughter infertile; the father wishing, if she bears a child, that the child 'live, and be a thwart disnatured torment to her.' Renaissance imprecation had a long tradition (Seneca, Latin tragic curses), but Shakespeare usually holds back from wishing specific bodily harm on daughters. Here he lets Lear go all the way. Some critics argue this speech forfeits Lear the audience's unqualified sympathy and is meant to; others that the theatrical force of grief requires the moral transgression. Either reading acknowledges the scene as a moral threshold — after this, audience sympathy for Lear is more costly.

trials.prompt: What is the historical 'licensed fool' tradition the Fool inherits?
options: A court office permitting the jester to speak truths that would be fatal from anyone else — the Fool's 'license' is literal social permission

trials.prompt: Of whom does Lear say 'How sharper than a serpent's tooth it is / To have a thankless child!'?
options: Goneril, whose reduction of his retinue has triggered his first great curse against filial ingratitude

trials.prompt: Why does the Fool offer Kent a coxcomb for following Lear?
options: The Fool's jest — anyone who still serves a king who has given away his kingdom is a fool; the coxcomb is the badge of folly he thinks Kent has earned

trials.prompt: What structural role is Albany playing in 1.4 that pays off in Acts 4–5?
options: He begins as the silent, hesitating husband whose moral weight accumulates through the play until he is among the final judges in 5.3
He is secretly working with Edmund

trials.prompt: What is dramatically risky about Lear's curse on Goneril's womb?
options: It crosses a moral threshold — wishing a daughter infertile, or her child to torment her — that some critics argue forfeits Lear's unqualified sympathy; audience feeling for him becomes costlier afterward
